speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson in Mount Kisco, New York, tape two. I'm Leslie [? Fass. ?] Mr. Zelikson, you were speaking about, before, your education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that you attended cheder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- actually, it's in his own house, the rabbi. The rabbi was Itche.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Itche Berkowitz. And he was very strict. We were but six students, actual small kids. We started to go to cheder when we were six years old. And he used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And if you didn't do the way he wanted to do, you had to put out your hand on the table. And he used to take a twig. And he'd [? strike ?] over the fingers. And if you said ouch, then he hits you more, until you didn't say nothing. And then you had to take the finger and point and say it over and over and over again until you did it correct. And he used to keep us there for quite a long time. If we wanted to go out, play, and do something else, but he says, you're in the cheder now. And you're going to stay until you learn. But when I was nine years old, I already knew all the five [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And we already learned [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That is unusual, because [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], you learn when you're about 11. But [INAUDIBLE], he taught us very well. And we were good students. And we learned. And that's why all my Christian friends says, you're too smart for me. I can't play with you, because you're way ahead in school. I says, I'm not smart, I just wanted to learn. And we went to school. And after school was finished, we had do our studies and do our homework. And we sit down. And before we went, we had to go out to play, we had to do all our homework that my father and mother sure that we do our homework. And we, we started to wear a hat. And my father, mother, and my family was proud of us, all of us. As a matter of fact, my older brother, he's supposed to become a lawyer. And my other two brothers, they want to become doctors. But unfortunate, we didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you want to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I liked to read books a lot. And I was interest in politics, believe it or not. I like politics. I used to ask my father what goes on in the world, what-- but my father never told, told me to be interested in politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were talking before about June, 1941, when the Germans were landing in Latvia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And subsequently, you were in a cemetery hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you also described the incident where you were asked to make a fire for a German in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a German or a Latvian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your father was shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they used to call them the Hitlerites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what type of garments did you wear? Did you have peyas and a long robe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tzitzits?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had [HEBREW SPEECH], yes, and [HEBREW SPEECH] but not peyas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father, he wasn't a Orthodox. He was a conservative. My grandfather was Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was shot in front of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened right then, after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I froze. Since then, I can't cry, no matter what. You could put knives in my ha-- hand or feet. It wouldn't bother me, I can't cry. And I froze. I couldn't believe that my father is dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I told my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how, how she reacted?

speaker: interviewee
text: She passed out, she fainted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers start to cry. They didn't even let us go to the funeral, because they took with the cart. And they pulled him away. They-- two Jews-- they threw him in a wagon. We call it a cart. And they moved them away. I don't even know where he was buried, because they didn't let us see. And after that, like I said, I went-- I worked for the German. And he asked me if I knew somebody. And I told him, I have my mother. And he let us come to work for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name, the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred.

speaker: interviewer
text: His last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Hesse. Yes. And he was an Obersturmfuhrer. It's equal to, I think, a major.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in charge of the Dvinsk ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And, well, actually, they putted the, the Latvian in charge of the Dvinsk ghetto, [INAUDIBLE]. You see, the [INAUDIBLE] were worst than the German. They begged the Germans-- give me back the rifle. I'll shoot the Jew. You don't have to shoot him. And yeah, the most important thing what I forgot-- they took the men and women-- the pregnant women and the little babies, they put on the second floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German and the Latvian police, the [INAUDIBLE]. And they told us everything that we have, like suitcases or baggage, to leave outside. And they put us in different rooms. And upstairs, I saw about six or eight German going up upstairs. And I saw they opened the windows while I was lighting the fire for the guard. I watched it. And downstairs was maybe about 12 Germans. And they took their rifles and put bayonets on their rifles. And then I saw the other Germans, the Nazis, throw out babies-- little babies. They were crying. And picked them up on bayonets. And there was a lot of dogs. And they threw the babies to the dogs. And everybody start to cry. And the women were screaming. And anybody that cried, they used to take the rifle-- the butt-- the end of the rifle-- and hit with the butt that nobody should cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this while you were making a fire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. While I was lighting the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is this after your father was shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then they told us to everybody to go out and stand at attention, like an appell. And a big German-- a Gruppenfuhrer-- came. And he started the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewee
text: He [? starten-- ?] he started up to a [INAUDIBLE], who goes left and who goes right. And I saw that he started to take a ruler and measure the height. Don't ask me why I did it, but I took off my shoes. And instead to stand in the shoe, I stood on top of the shoe, which made it me couple of millimeter taller. And he start to measure. And he start to go left, right, left, right. And he measured me. But he didn't notice that I was standing on the shoes. And he told me to right. And my mother and my three brothers-- he motioned to the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw it. So I start to run to my mother and brothers. He grabbed me by the neck. And he says, where you going? I says, I'm going with my mother and three brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he hit me with the butt of the rifle. And he says, you dirty Jew. You not going to do anything. You do where I tell you to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your oldest brother? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: My three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was your oldest brother shorter than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, he was taller. But for some reason, he moved him to the left. He-- at that time, my brother was sick. He, he kept on coughing a lot. And they eliminate right away the sick, the old, and the very young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went to the left. And at that time, I didn't know what it meant-- left or right. But then I find out left meant to death and right to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I ran to them and they-- he grabbed me. And he hit me. He says, you going to go where I tell you to go. And he put me to the right. And then, we got separate. The people that went left, they put in a different block. And the people that went right went a different block. And the people went right, they gave us a little book a red card, a red card that means you were a worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone there with you that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Was a little older than me-- Chaim. And he says, we are surviving because we, we got red card. And my mother says that she's working for the German officer. So they took my mother from my brothers away. And they give her a red card. And my, my brothers, they moved to the block where all the people went to the left. And I worked with my mother for about a month. And the German started to trust us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who you worked for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Obersturmf-- Mr. Hesse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred.

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Hesse. So he, he let us take care of the house by ourself. And I says to my mother, now we have a chance to escape. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, did you know where you brothers were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know they were in the ghetto-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living in the house you took care of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we weren't living. We were living in the ghetto. But we were separate from my brothers. We were on the right side. And they were on the left side. We couldn't get in touch with them. We couldn't go near them, because they put guards there. And my, my mother says to me, we're going to escape. So the first thing I thought about it, to go back to Viski. It was 20 kilometer from Dvinsk, where I was born. And we saw-- at that time, it was maybe around 8:00 o'clock at night. We went to the marketplace in Viski. And we saw a couple of trucks and a lot of German and Latvian police. And on the market, there was a lot of Jews, maybe a couple of thousand at least. And the marketplace had only two ways to go out and go in. And both ways was blocked off. And my mother says, let's hide. Because if they'll find us, they'll put us in there, too. We didn't know what was going on. And then the German opened the trucks. The canvas came up from the truck. And there was big machine guns. And they moved away-- the, the German-- and they started shoot. And all the people fell all over. My mother says, we better run from here. And I know the farmer-- the farmer was Mackay-- a big farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you standing when you witnessed this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was right by the marketplace, hiding behind a wall. And we saw it. So we ran away from the marketplace. And we went to Mackay's farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what season was this? Do you remember what month this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- it was pretty cold. So it would have to be in December.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It would have to be December. And we saw the market-- the farm. And they had-- in the barn-- had three little glass. And they had maybe a couple of nails holding the glass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I pulled out, with my fingers, the nails. And I opened the barn-- the door. And we went in. And we slept for two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the third day, I tried to take out the glass again. And it fell out from my hand. And it broke. And it was noise. It was a big commotion. And the farmer started to-- with his helpers-- to look us. My mother says, let's go underneath the hay. And it was a big stack of hay. We went all the way underneath. And we heard like pitchforks digging in. My mother fell on top of me. She wanted to make sure that I doesn't-- I wouldn't get killed. And she covered me with more hay. And they kept on sticking with the pitchforks. But they didn't go deep enough. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't harmed, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we, we weren't harmed. But my mother says, we can't stay no more here. We got to run away. Because they're going to find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went-- we want to go back to Dvinsk. A woman saw us walking. And she start to scream, you dirty Jew. And she called the Latvia police.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us to Dvinsk jail. In Dvinsk jail, they put us in a cell. Maybe it was three feet by six feet, no more. And the windows was covered. They had covers. You can only see on the ground, but you can't see in front of you or upstairs. It was a, a big cover-- covers the windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the Gestapo start to ask me questions. Do you know where the Jews are hiding? Did you know-- where you come from, you, you must hid some Jews. So where they hiding? I told him, I'm, I'm a little boy. I don't know nothing about it. And he start to hit me. Where did he hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He first hit me in the face And then on my shoulders. And then he took out a little black whip. And on the end, had lead. And he start to hit me over the head. And I didn't know. Because I told him, I don't know. We were only me and my mother. He, he told me I'm lying. Then he put me against a wall. And there was mud-- actual mud on the wall. And he put me against it. He said, you better tell me the truth. I said, I'm telling you the truth. And as I was stood against the wall, I heard zzzzzzzzz. And my whole body went like on a fire. And I didn't know what it was. He says, if you, if you lie, I'll do it more. I'll put more electricity. At that time, I didn't know what the electricity was. And he did it quite a few times. But I couldn't tell him anything. Then he took toothpicks-- little sticks-- and put it under my fingernails and lit a match. And it kept on burning. I kept on screaming. And he told me I'm lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, I'm not lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you crying?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was screaming. I couldn't cry. I, I can't cry today. I went by the best doctors. And they say I have it locked in. Since I saw my father dead, it, it's locked in inside me and cannot be opened. I went to specialists and that, but I can't cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put matches--

speaker: interviewee
text: They put matches. And he was wondering why I don't cry. I was screaming but not crying. And I told him, I-- I'm telling the truth. I'm not lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, they, they stopped. Then they put me in a place-- you can't sit, you can't stand, and you can't lay down. It's built-- can I get up? I just want to show you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- it was like like this. And they kept on dripping water on my head. And since then, my head has a hole in it, right here. I'll show you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to turn around now?

speaker: interviewee
text: See this?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and we were there a month. After a month, they told us to clean the, the street in the jail, alongside the, the jail, to clean the street. And for couple of weeks, they, they trusted us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me and my mother, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: In jail-- we were in jail. I find out later that my mother was there. Because I thought they took her someplace else. But when she went to clean the street and I went to clean the street, we met. And we did it for a couple of weeks. And they trusted us. And my mother says, we're going to escape again. And sure, we tried to escape. We got caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom? By the Latvian. The [? Einsatz. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the [? Einsatz. ?] And they took us back to Dvinsk ghetto. In Dvinsk ghetto, they told us we're going to stay here. But then I find out that 6:00 o'clock in the morning, they were taking us on trucks. And they were going to shoot us in [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] used to be a resort place where all the rich people used to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: By, by the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Beautiful place. But there was a big forest. And me and my mother was in the same freight train. There was a couple of hundred in each freight train, a big train. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. They took you from the Dvinsk ghetto by trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trucks to the train-- and they put us in a freight train and locked us in with the freight train. And it had just little windows in, in the car. And when the freight train came right by [INAUDIBLE], by the forest, it slowed down. So my mother-- the, the train was making a turn like a half a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Curve.

speaker: interviewee
text: Half a curve-- and it slowed down. My mother pushed me out through that little window. And then she jumped. We didn't get hurt. We jumped out. And we were about five minutes away from the forest. And we saw the train stopped. And all the people came out from the train. They pushed them out. And they told them to take off all their clothes and leave the clothes. And they went naked. And then we saw-- about maybe 10 minutes away from the forest-- we saw big graves, gigantic graves. There was four graves. I'll never forget it. And they told the people to stay alongside the grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: They walked the people from the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the people-- they didn't walk. They forced them. They, they hit them with the, the stakes and with the butt of the rifles to go. Schnell, schnell!

speaker: interviewer
text: They told them to go fast by the grave. And-- but I was surprised that nobody turned back, you know? Nobody was afraid to go against the, even if they have rifles. If it was me, I w-- I would try to do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, they didn't. And I saw, again, the big trucks. And they took off the canvas. And all the people that stood by the grave-- they start to shoot. And they were falling into the graves. And some other slave laborer was covering the graves with sand. And the, the graves kept on moving. So the man was in charge-- the Obersturmfuhrer-- he said, the devils. Even if we kill them, they still alive. He says, everybody-- all the soldiers-- take the rifles, put the bayonets. And wherever you see the grave move, stick it. And they kept on doing it. But the grave still kept on moving. He says, even if they're dead, we can't kill them. And then my mother saw-- she says take a look. They're putting white sand-- I never saw white sand-- on the, on the graves. They're putting all over white sand. And after a while, the graves stopped moving. And then later, we find out that was lime. They poured lime so make sure that everybody is dead. And I was separate from-- I walked away in the forest. I had to go to relieve myself. And I walked. And I didn't find the right place. So I went a little further up. I thought, I'm not going to get lost. And I walked away. And I got lost. And I got separate from my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.
